title: Invalidation Propagation
use_for: defining how local changes invalidate downstream stage outputs
controlling recompute scope through explicit propagation rules
stopping pipeline work at clean boundaries
rules: propagate invalidation only as far as derived products are actually affected
stop work at the first clean boundary
make invalidation rules explicit and inspectable
prefer local dirty scope over whole-pipeline reruns
procedure: List source changes and affected products.
Map which downstream stages each change invalidates.
Define clean boundaries where work can stop.
Verify propagation cost is lower than broad recompute.

primary_when: the request is specifically about dirty propagation, invalidation scope, or selective pipeline recompute
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about stage splitting or raw execution order

solves: how far a change should propagate through the pipeline
how to avoid rerunning unaffected stages

core_principles: Invalidation is an architectural path, not an ad hoc optimization patch.
Only affected products should force downstream work.
Clean boundaries should be as explicit as dirty ones.
constraints: Propagation rules must be reviewable per stage.
Dirty logic must not cost more than the work it skips.
decision_rules: Use targeted invalidation when a local source change affects only a subset of stage outputs.
Use broad invalidation only when structural or global state truly changes.
Attach incremental-recompute when invalidation must drive a bounded partial-rerun path instead of stopping at dirtiness description.

avoid_patterns: implicit invalidation through shared mutable state

recipes.name: Stop recompute at a clean stage boundary
recipes.when: Use when a local source change does not affect all downstream products.
steps: Mark only products affected by the source change dirty.
Propagate to dependent stages only if their inputs changed.
Stop once a stage's retained outputs remain valid.

pitfalls: Hiding invalidation through broad shared state.
Paying more for dirty checks than recomputation.
review_checklist: Can invalidation scope be explained per change type?
Do clean boundaries stop work early?
Is dirty logic cheaper than broad recompute?
Are stage dependencies explicit enough for propagation review?

references.title: Local Reference: Invalidation Propagation
references.interpretation: Use this first for selective recompute rules and clean-boundary stopping logic.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for invalidation and partial recompute.
